metadata.seed: It's like an aptitude test. It has, like, math problems on it and, like, scenarios in order for me to move up to the assistant manager. Precision.

patient.query: It's like an aptitude test. It has, like, math problems on it and, like, scenarios in order for me to move up to the assistant manager. Precision.

automatic_thoughts: I need to perform well
What if I fail?
I must be precise

entailment: Demonstrates knowledge of expected responses to operative procedure (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re feeling a lot of pressure to get everything exactly right. What does “precision” mean to you in this situation, and how do you know it’s the only way to succeed? Have you noticed any moments when a small mistake didn’t actually derail your progress?

patient.query: Yeah, I guess I do feel a lot of pressure. For me, precision means not making any mistakes, like if I can just get everything perfect, then maybe I won’t feel so anxious. But then, when I think back, I can remember moments where things didn’t go as planned, and I survived. Still, it’s hard to shake the feeling that one little slip can change everything.

triggers: moments where things didn’t go as planned
automatic_thoughts: precision means not making any mistakes
if I can just get everything perfect, then maybe I won’t feel so anxious
one little slip can change everything

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re carrying a lot of weight on the idea that everything has to be flawless. What evidence do you have that a small slip could actually change everything for you? When you look back at those moments that didn’t go as planned, how did you feel afterward, and what did you learn from them?

patient.query: It’s true, I do feel like I’m holding on to this idea of perfection tightly. But sometimes I think about those moments and... it’s confusing. I felt embarrassed or frustrated when things went wrong, but I didn’t fall apart like I worried I would. I guess I learned that I can bounce back, but that worry still sticks around. It’s like… I want to avoid that discomfort at all costs, even if it’s not as bad as I imagine.

automatic_thoughts: I need to be perfect
I will fall apart
I want to avoid discomfort

neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: I hear how tightly you’re holding that perfection standard and how the fear of discomfort still lingers even after you’ve bounced back. What evidence do you have that the discomfort you feel is truly worse than it actually turns out to be? How might you test the idea that avoiding discomfort at all costs is the safest way to protect yourself?

patient.query: I mean, I know on some level that my fears usually don’t match up with reality, but it’s hard to remind myself of that in the moment. I often think about how I felt after I messed up and it just sticks with me. Testing that idea feels risky, you know? Like, what if I try and it really is as awful as I fear? I guess I just want to feel safe and in control, but I know that’s not always possible. It’s like this

automatic_thoughts: my fears usually don’t match up with reality
I felt after I messed up
testing that idea feels risky
what if I try and it really is as awful as I fear

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re worried that trying something new could lead to losing control. What evidence do you have that it will turn out as badly as you fear? Can you think of any times when you tried something and it didn’t end up that awful?

patient.query: Yeah, I guess there have been times where I tried something I was nervous about and it wasn't a disaster. But my mind tends to jump to the worst-case scenarios, you know? I remember one time I gave a presentation and I thought I would totally flop, but it turned out okay. Still, even that moment feels like a fluke sometimes, like luck played a bigger role than my own ability. So it's hard to really trust that things might not go badly again.

it feels like a fluke
luck played a bigger role than my ability
things might go badly again

neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’ve had some successes, but you still feel like they were flukes. What evidence do you have that your preparation and skill played a role in those moments? How might you view those experiences differently if you considered your own effort as a key factor?

patient.query: Yeah, I mean, I did prepare a lot for those presentations, and I guess I should recognize that. But it’s like my brain just brushes aside those efforts and focuses on the times it didn’t go well. I think if I acknowledged my work, it would feel like giving myself credit I’m not used to giving. I’m just worried that if I rely on that view, I might set myself up for disappointment. It feels safer to keep beating myself up, even though I know it

I’m scared that if I let my guard down, I’ll just set myself up for a bigger fall. So, even the thought of giving myself credit feels risky, like maybe I’m j

patient.query: Yeah, I guess that does make sense. I mean, I’ve had some times where I felt good about achievements, but... it’s like there’s this little voice that reminds me of the flipside. Like, after I felt proud of something, I sometimes ended up in a situation where I struggled again, and it would feel like I was back at square one. So maybe I focus more on what could go wrong than what went right, you know? But it’s exhausting to carry that

automatic_thoughts: there's a flipside
I focus more on what could go wrong

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re carrying a lot of weight after each success. What evidence do you have that the next challenge will undo what you’ve achieved? How might you notice the successes you’ve earned, even when difficulties arise?

patient.query: I don’t know, it just feels like the stakes keep getting higher each time. I guess I’m worried that one mistake will overshadow everything I’ve worked for, like it’ll prove that I’m not good enough. It’s hard to see successes when my mind keeps pulling me back to what went wrong or what could go wrong next. I want to celebrate those moments, but that fear keeps creeping in, making everything feel tentative.

automatic_thoughts: one mistake will overshadow everything
I’m not good enough
hard to see successes

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re carrying a lot of pressure that a single slip could undo everything. One way to look at it is that a mistake is just one event in a long series of efforts—does it really have that much power over your overall progress? If you could view it as a learning moment rather than a verdict on your worth, how might that shift how you celebrate your successes?

patient.query: I mean, I get what you’re saying, and it makes sense. But it’s hard to really feel that way in the moment. I think part of me still believes that one mistake does reflect who I am, like it confirms my fears about not being enough. Shifting my perspective seems like a nice idea, but it feels so far from where I am right now. I just worry that if I start to celebrate, it’ll make me more vulnerable to falling again, and that scares me

it confirms my fears about not being enough
shifting my perspective feels far from where I am
celebrating makes me more vulnerable to falling again
